## Blockchain networks are peer-to-peer systems, not client-server services

Up to Lecture 5, students have seen how a blockchain can represent valid
transactions, ordered blocks, and a consensus rule. The next question is how those
objects move through a decentralized system in the first place.

Blockchain networks are not usually organized as one central server broadcasting the
truth to everyone else. They are peer-to-peer systems in which many nodes store
ledger data, validate messages locally, and connect to multiple other nodes.

## Why the peer-to-peer structure matters

That structure changes both the strengths and the weaknesses of the system.

- There is no single server whose failure shuts the whole network down.
- Nodes can validate independently rather than trusting one coordinator.
- Censorship becomes harder because many paths may exist for message relay.
- On the other hand, nodes may temporarily disagree because not every message reaches
  every peer at the same time.

This is why consistency in blockchain is a network-level achievement, not just a data
structure property.

## What a node is doing

At an introductory level, a node is a participant that:

- stores some view of the ledger state,
- checks transactions and blocks against protocol rules,
- sends and receives messages from peers,
- and updates its local state as new information arrives.

Different production systems have different node roles, but the first teaching model
should stay simple: many independently checking peers, no single trusted hub.

## Why propagation delays matter

Decentralization does not eliminate timing problems. If one valid block reaches some
nodes before others, the network can briefly disagree about the current tip. That is
one reason forks and stale blocks occur even when participants are honest.

Students should connect this back to the consensus lecture: consensus rules resolve
competing histories, but networking determines how quickly information about those
histories spreads.

## What students should be able to explain

After this concept, students should be able to explain:

- how a blockchain peer-to-peer network differs from a client-server model,
- why multiple peer connections support resilience and censorship resistance,
- what a node does beyond merely storing data,
- and why propagation delay matters for forks and coordination.
